intro:
The purpose of God is his eternal, sovereign, and wise plan according to which he freely and unchangeably ordains and accomplishes all that comes to pass, to the praise of his own glory. Scripture speaks of it as his counsel (Hebrew ‘etsah) and his purpose (Greek prothesis, boule) — not a wish that may fail nor a reaction to events, but a settled design conceived in eternity and certain to stand. Where his decrees are the particular determinations of his will, his purpose is the one comprehensive intention that binds them into a single story.

The Old Testament grounds the believer’s confidence in the unfailing firmness of this purpose. “I am God, and there is no other… declaring the end from the beginning… saying, ‘My counsel shall stand, and I will accomplish all my purpose’” (Isaiah 46:9–10). “The counsel of the LORD stands forever, the plans of his heart to all generations” (Psalm 33:11). Human scheming cannot overturn it: “Many are the plans in the mind of a man, but it is the purpose of the LORD that will stand” (Proverbs 19:21); “the LORD of hosts has purposed, and who will annul it?” (Isaiah 14:27).

In the New Testament this purpose is unveiled as centered wholly in Christ. God made known “the mystery of his will, according to his purpose, which he set forth in Christ… to unite all things in him, things in heaven and things on earth” (Ephesians 1:9–10), working all things “according to the counsel of his will” (Ephesians 1:11). It is “the eternal purpose that he has realized in Christ Jesus our Lord” (Ephesians 3:11) — a grace “given us in Christ Jesus before the ages began” (2 Timothy 1:9). Even the greatest evil ever done served it: Christ was delivered up “according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God” (Acts 2:23).

Rightly understood, the purpose of God is neither cold fatalism nor a charge that makes God the author of sin. The same verse that names the cross as God’s “definite plan” also says, “you crucified and killed” by lawless hands (Acts 2:23): his sovereign purpose is accomplished through real means and genuine human responsibility, never apart from his holiness. His purpose is wise, good, and unfrustrated — “I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be thwarted” (Job 42:2).

For faith this doctrine is bedrock comfort and a call to worship. Because God works “all things” together for good to those “called according to his purpose” (Romans 8:28), the believer’s salvation rests not on shifting circumstance but on an eternal, Christ-centered plan that cannot fail. It steadies the soul in suffering, fuels confident prayer and mission, and bends the whole course of history toward its appointed end: the summing-up of all things in Christ, to the glory of God.
